data.title: Fidelity Real Estate Investment Portfolio Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

For the third quarter of 2025, the U.S. real estate investment trust market, as measured by the MSCI U.S. IMI Real Estate 25/50 Linked Index, returned 3.76%, lagging the 8.12% gain of the U.S. stock market, according to the S&P 500® index. Economically sensitive property segments – such as regional malls (+18%), industrial (+7%), office (+7%) and lodging real estate (+6%) investment trusts – outperformed in this environment, given the companies' more cyclical cash flows and as the market proved more accommodating toward property types tied to near-term economic growth.

In contrast, residential REITs – which includes apartment (-4%) and single-family rental (-9%) – and telecom tower REITs (-11%) were among the relative laggards, reflecting investors' collective expectation that cash flows may not accelerate in the current market environment. The underperformance of residential real estate also reflected market participants' concern about a near-term excess in supply, particularly in the Sun Belt region, where significant building activity has taken place. However, property fundamentals in the office real estate segment appear to have bottomed, with "green shoots" appearing in leasing activity.

Meanwhile, the U.S. equity market was strong in Q3, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April. The advance has been supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reduction since December. Growth stocks continued to lead in Q3, while smaller-cap shares outpaced large-caps, signaling a broadening of market leadership and increased investor appetite for risk. Renewed expectations for Fed rate cuts also supported stocks. On September 17, the central bank lowered its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points, shifting from its wait-and-see stance. The decision reflected concerns about labor-market softness, which outweighed a rise in inflation – up 2.9% year-over-year in August, the highest reading since January. As of September 30, the market expects monetary easing into 2026 and beyond. ■

Performance Review

For the third quarter, the fund gained 0.89%, significantly trailing the 3.76% advance of the benchmark, the MSCI U.S. IMI Real Estate 25/50 Linked Index.

Versus the real estate industry index, security selection in the industrial REITs segment meaningfully detracted. In this group, an overweight stake in Americold Realty Trust (-25%), an owner and operator of warehouse properties specializing in the cold storage of frozen and fresh foods, was a particularly notable detractor. Lower-than-expected utilization of the firm's facilities weighed on the stock. Meanwhile, the fund's overweight in Prologis (+10%) – the world's largest industrial REIT and the No. 1 portfolio holding as of September 30 – partly offset the negative security selection within the industrial category. Picks within retail REITs also detracted from the fund's relative performance. Most notably, lacking exposure to strong-performing mall owner Simon Property Group (+18%) weighed on the fund's relative result. Instead, we chose to gain most of our retail exposure through shopping centers, which we saw as a superior business to malls. Stock picking and an overweight in the telecom tower category further hampered results, especially an overweight stake in American Tower (-12%) – the fund's No. 2 holding on September 30. As relatively defensive investments, tower stocks largely lagged their more economically sensitive counterparts this quarter.

On the positive side, the fund benefited from a relative overweight in Compass (+27%), which operates an online real estate brokerage platform. This company benefited from strong operational management, high agent productivity and the effective use of technology to boost business. We reduced the fund's allocation to this stock in Q3. Also boosting the fund's relative result was a lack of exposure to apartment REIT AvalonBay Communities (-4%). Apartment REITs were relative underperformers in Q3, reflecting the market's apparent view that cash flows for these REITs may not accelerate in the current economic environment. ■

Outlook and Positioning

Our investment strategy in Q3 emphasized our best stock picking ideas based on individual company fundamentals and valuations. As of September 30, modest outsized exposure to apartment REITs (9%) reflected our view that the industry is well positioned over the long term. Even in the heavily built Sunbelt region, we believe that new supply is likely to decline over time. We also believe this trend is likely to persist, given higher construction costs, restrictive immigration policies leading to rising labor rates and massive demand for data centers that is further distorting labor supply. All told, it's adding delays to the construction process, which could further constrain supply and push up rents over time.

Elsewhere, we maintained substantial exposure to industrial REITs (16% of the fund at quarter end and a sizable overweight) due to strong demand for specialized distribution facilities, driven in part by rapid growth in e-commerce. Although industrial REITs encountered difficulty earlier in 2025, we expect to see occupancy rates start to tick up as the supply/demand balance improves. The positive impact of this reduction will take time to play out, but we believe it ultimately could be quite beneficial to industrial REIT cash flows, as property owners will need higher rental rates to economically justify new construction. Another area of opportunity that we continue to see is the assisted-living industry, which is benefiting from a favorable combination of strong demand and constrained supply. In Q3, the fund's allocation to health care REITs was 12%, up from about 11% at midyear. Meanwhile, the fund maintained limited exposure to mall owners, given the significant long-term business challenges they face.

However, we did see attractive opportunities among retail strip-center REITs and, as such, maintained an overweight in the industry. As we see it, owners of strip centers have better businesses than mall owners, benefiting from favorable demand and more limited supply of attractive locations. The fund is overweight data centers – which made up about 12% of the fund on September 30. It's become increasingly challenging to satisfy the demand for data centers due to constraints on new supply, which include access to power and water resources and the scarcity of optimal locations. Many customers want to be close to the internet's on- and off ramps to make their data connections faster. Thus, well-located data centers are seeing strong growth in rental income. This situation is creating tremendous pricing power for the industry. In seeking data center industry investments, we've prioritized REITs that operate in desirable locations close to their customers, as well as those with stronger financial positions and greater access to capital.

Our outlook for REITs is generally positive. Market participants are anticipating several additional cuts to short-term interest rates – a positive factor if it also leads to lower long-term rates and, as a result, the cost of capital. In addition, after the REIT market's significant underperformance in recent years, valuations have become much more attractive relative to the broader equity market. In short, we're relatively optimistic about REITs, though we're also mindful of how growth-oriented equities seemingly continue to go up daily. In this environment, one should remember that REITs have historically been good diversifiers of a growth-heavy portfolio. All told, we're going to continue to pay close attention to the broader economic context in which the fund operates, while focusing our attention on bottom-up stock picking. ■

Fidelity® Real Estate Investment Portfolio

Investment Approach

Fidelity® Real Estate Investment Portfolio seeks above-average income and long-term capital growth, consistent with reasonable investment risk, by investing in securities of companies that own and, in most cases, operate commercial real estate properties.

Investment in real estate securities has the potential to provide portfolio diversification, consistent income generation, total return and the ability to outpace inflation.

We believe real estate investment trusts (REITS) represent a balance between real estate and stocks, and that recognizing attributes of both is key to identifying opportunities to outperform.

Through rigorous bottom-up research from both Fidelity's dedicated real estate team and the firm's broader research resources, we strive to add value through security selection within a disciplined risk framework. Our process seeks to determine the relative attractiveness of individual REITs and will try to take advantage of pricing discrepancies in the market.

data.title: Starfighters Space: A Hypersonic And Space Launch Hype To Sell
data.description:
Starfighters Space, Inc. (FJET) went public earlier this month, raising $40 million at $3.59 per share, including pre-sold common shares. The stock price subsequently rocketed to $31.50 and currently trades at $12.40 after the stock lost 60% of its value. In this report, I discuss the company’s operations, the IPO, and why I believe Starfighters Space is a typical name that has substantial risk attached despite space and hypersonic opportunities.

Starfighter Space: A Different View On Space Launch Systems

The conventional way to bring satellites into orbit is by launching a rocket from the ground consisting of various stages, and some of these stages are reusable, thereby lowering launch costs. Starfighter Space envisions a very different approach by utilizing F-104 supersonic jets that fly to 45,000 feet, release a rocket, which can be used to inject a microsatellite into orbit using the Starlaunch I vehicle and later on Small-sats using the Starlaunch II. The company expects that this air-launched method will substantially reduce launch costs per kilogram compared to peers. Lowering the cost-burden is a key requirement to unlock opportunities in space, and Starfighters Space aims to do that using the air-launch method.

At the same time, we should note that while this may lower costs and increase flexibility, prior attempts by Virgin Orbit to provide air-launched solutions have ended in failure, with the company ultimately collapsing. So, air-launched solutions are not new, nor is there a particularly successful track record, and that also does mean that I consider Starfighters Space to be a higher-risk investment for their space business. The company also quotes launch cost per kilogram using the Starlaunch II and it is more than likely that Starlaunch I costs per kilogram are worse than Starlaunch II costs. At a 100-kilogram payload to LEO, it is sized substantially smaller than Electron’s 150 kilogram, but it is not quite known whether there is any substantial demand for the payload class, nor is anything about the business concept proven. Rocket Lab’s (RKLB) Electron rocket enjoys a good track record of launch success and precision on orbit injection. I believe a major risk is that the Starlaunch I is not cost competitive, and the Starlaunch II initially may also not be cost competitive.

Besides air-launched satellite deployment, Starfighters Space offers pilot training for the F-104. The more promising part of the business, in my view, is the participation opportunities in hypersonic rocket testing for which the company has partnered with the Air Force Research Lab. Starfighters Space still has to prove its business case, grow a backlog, and realize target costs and revenues for profitability and mission success.

A Challenging Path For Starfighters Space

The IPO is labeled as if it raised $40 million, and that is only partially correct. The company started raising funds in September 2024, and prior to the IPO, it had issued around 5 million shares, leaving 6.15 million shares to be issued. The IPO in December resulted in gross proceeds of $22.1 million and $20.45 million in net proceeds. The company expected net proceeds of $36.2 million (Prospectus Page 42), $8 million is earmarked for CapEx, $4 million for Starlaunch related R&D, and $0.77 million for loan repayment.

The company’s expenditures have been loaded into the second half of 2025, with $12 million allocated to airplane acquisition, $1.5 million for a drop test to acquire a launch license from the FAA, and $5 million to acquire permission to launch. That brings the total backloaded costs to $18.5 million. So, much of the raised capital actually already has been assigned.

According to the cash flow statement, the annualized operating cash burn is $6.7 million. The company ended Q3 with $3.9 million in cash. Starfighters Space is currently in the development phase, meaning that it is generating no revenues and producing substantial operating losses. Starfighters Space also converted debt to equity on the 17th and 18th of December. I have mapped the pro forma cash position excluding the non-recurring costs, and that shows that there is a $23.4 million cash position. That does seem to be quite comfortable against the $6.7 million annualized cash burn. However, if we include the higher cost profile as the company needs to make investments to accelerate commercialization, we see that against expected costs to be carried this year, the year-end cash balance will only be $4.1 million.

Essentially that means that substantially more capital is needed, which increases the likelihood of further dilution. Currently, the stock is trading at around $12.40 while the cash per share by year-end is expected to be $0.14. Assuming the lack of substantial operating revenues and activities, the cash per share closely resembles the book value per share. Aerospace and defense peers trade at 3.7x P/B. A company such as Rocket Labs has a median of 5x while it currently trades at around 30 times its book value. A peer group valuation gives a $0.52 price target, while the RKLB median gives $0.70 and the current multiple gives a $4.20 target.

If we assume that the company will raise around $17.5 million to cover normalized cash usage for the coming two years, we get to $0.55 in cash per share, which, against the previously discussed multiples, is a price target of $2-$2.78 for the peer group and RKLB median and $16.65 against the current P/B for Rocket Lab. The current share price is $12.40, and I strongly believe that does not reflect the cash intensity in the quarters ahead and the unproven business model for Starfighters Space. As a result, I am marking the stock a Strong Sell.

Conclusion: Starfighters Space Stock Is Not Attractive At This Point

I believe that the Starfighters Space business concept is not an attractive one. On paper, the company can cover micro- and small satellite launches at a substantially lower launch cost per kg. However, we note that air launches have not been particularly successful, as they are also not easy to execute. Virgin Orbit, which used a bigger airplane and a bigger launcher, ended up failing as a flexible air-launch provider, and Starfighters Space has no proven track record on mission success and financial targets and timelines. The capital raise allows FJET to ramp up its efforts, but I believe additional capital raises are required, and consequently I do not believe there is an attractive investment angle for the company.

data.title: Japan Is The Canary In The Global Debt Coal Mine
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Investors are beginning to zero in on what is happening in Japan.

For decades, Japan sat in a world of near-zero rates, suppressed volatility, and chronic deflation, but that has quickly changed.

In a previous macro post, I discussed exactly how the yen carry unwind could threaten global markets.

But this is just one side of the coin.

The problem with Japan is structural debt and inflation, an issue which could soon plague other developed markets, too, including the US

Japan may just be the first domino to fall.

But what does this mean for investors?

The Yield Signal Investors Are Watching

Japanese yields are on the rise.

Some investors believe that if Japanese yields were ever to fully converge with or even exceed U.S. yields, this could "break" global bond markets.

But why?

Japan has built its entire fiscal and monetary framework on the assumption of permanently low interest rates. This monetary experiment may be coming to an end.

Japan’s Debt Problem Is Structural, Not Cyclical

Japan’s debt sits at over 200% of GDP.

Meanwhile, in the U.S., debt to GDP is closer to 120%.

While both figures are historically elevated and problematic, the difference in scale is critical.

A government services its debt through tax revenues generated by its economy. When debt grows far faster than economic output, even modest increases in interest rates can overwhelm fiscal capacity.

In Japan’s case, a significant portion of annual government spending already goes toward debt servicing. Higher yields quickly crowd out everything else.

Inflation: The Wrench In The Works

For more than 20 years, Japan struggled with deflation. This allowed the government to borrow aggressively at ultra-low rates with little immediate consequence.

That macro backdrop has now changed. Over the past three years, Japanese inflation has averaged roughly 3%.

While that number may look tame compared to recent U.S. or European experience, it is a profound shift for Japan.

Low inflation made aggressive monetary easing safe and ultimately sat at the heart of the now infamous Yen carry trade.

The Bank of Japan’s Dilemma

For years, the Bank of Japan acted as the buyer of last resort for Japanese government bonds, suppressing yields through massive quantitative easing. At its peak, the central bank owned a staggering share of the entire JGB market.

Once inflation appeared, this strategy became unsustainable, especially with the Yen weakening too.

Printing money to buy bonds in an inflationary environment risks currency instability and loss of confidence. As a result, the Bank of Japan has been forced to step back and, more recently, even reduce the size of its balance sheet.

Logically, when the largest buyer exits a market, prices fall, and yields rise.

Can Japan Grow Out of Its Debt?

This is the key question.

There is a credible argument that Japan could reduce its debt burden through inflation by growing nominal GDP, but the country faces some challenges.

Because Japan’s debt is overwhelmingly yen-denominated and domestically held, policymakers have more control than countries dependent on foreign capital.

If inflation could be sustained in the 2–3% range, nominal GDP growth would rise, tax revenues would increase, and the real value of existing debt would gradually erode.

But Japan’s structural growth is low due to an ageing population, weak productivity growth, and limited immigration.

Inflation without real growth risks raising costs without meaningfully expanding the tax base.

At the same time, rising bond yields threaten the entire strategy.

Japan can only inflate its way out if interest rates remain below nominal growth, and markets are now pushing back.

If yields rise too far, this could lead to renewed financial repression. The BoJ could start buying bonds again, or commercial banks could even be required to carry more sovereign debt.

Why This Matters for the United States

There are two key reasons why this matters to the U.S and U.S. investors.

Japan is the largest buyer of Treasuries, holding more than $1 trillion.

A destabilization of Japan’s bond markets could spillover into U.S. markets.

Namely, higher Japanese yields could encourage domestic investors to repatriate capital, selling U.S. Treasuries and buying JGBs.

This is an extension of the dynamics of the carry trade dynamics I discussed before.

But perhaps more important is the fact that Japan is basically showing us what the future could look like for the U.S.

While America isn't as indebted, deficits are a problem, and I do think that the government will try to reduce this debt burden through inflation.

Japan will be patient zero, as it tries to do just this.

  • Will they have to resort to more financial repression?

  • What does this even look like in a world with Gold and Bitcoin (BTC-USD).

  • And perhaps on a broader level, is there a way back from an ageing population operating below replacement rates?

What This Means For Investors

The market is already pricing in the endgame here.

The rally in gold (XAUUSD:CUR) in terms of U.S. dollars is nothing compared to the rally we have seen relative to the Japanese yen.

What’s interesting, though, is that even in the face of higher yields, the Nikkei (NKY:IND) has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500).

On the one hand, inflation has actually revived the Japanese economy, with hopes that wages and earnings will now begin to increase.

And of course, earnings will increase at least in nominal terms, as the yen keeps devaluing.

This is why, ultimately, as governments around the world try to crawl their way out of debt, assets, both hard and financial, will do well.

data.title: It Will Likely Be A Good Year For FSK And Its Yield-Investors
data.description:
FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK) was one of my first business development companies, or BDCs, that I included in my portfolio, back in late 2023. It was also one of the first BDCs that I covered on this platform, issuing a bullish article in December 2023. In that piece I compared FSK (at that time the second largest BDC) with Ares Capital (ARCC) (the largest BDC), and made a decision to go with the former due to a more compelling P/NAV multiple relative to the underlying NII generation.

Since then I was holding FSK in my portfolio all the time until January this year, when I published a thesis update piece. My decision was to harvest ~27% in total return profits and exit FSK due to a too marginal discount, which had then narrowed to only 7%. I just felt that the odds for an upside were much lower than seeing a leg down.

The timing was almost perfect. After that decision and until I decided to reopen the stake in FSK, the share price had dropped by ~33%.

If we measure returns from the moment when I deployed again capital into FSK (October 9, 2025), we will notice that the total return figure is almost 6%, which even slightly exceeds that of the overall BDC sector (BIZD).

However, as of now, FSK still trades at a very steep discount of 32%, which is more or less at the same level when I made my first move in FSK. I have to say that the situation looks quite attractive on a risk-adjusted basis due to exactly the massive discount. It is clear that the fundamentals are not the strongest out there for FSK, but I would argue that the disconnect is simply unjustified given how some other peers are priced (e.g., sector average is 0.86x).

Let's quickly review the case and see how the recent Q3 financials have impacted the story here.

Thesis review

I will immediately start with the challenges, which are quite many.

First, the NII per share declined in Q3 to $0.57 (down by $0.03 compared to the previous quarter). The key drivers were largely the same as for most other BDC peers - i.e., lower base rates and tighter spreads. In the earlier quarters of 2025, FSK was battling with rising non-accruals, which added an extra layer of pressure for the NII per share generation. In Q3, 2025, this component was well-managed.

Second, based on the most recent 10-Q filing, FSK has approximately 15% of income coming in the form of payment in kind. The sector average is 6.4% and for some top-notch quality BDCs the PIK gets even lower than that - e.g., for Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) and Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC). The reason why FSK has so meaningful exposure to PIK stems mostly from the multiple restructured loans, where the BDC has tailored the financings in order to avoid full losses.

Third, FSK's base dividend coverage stands at 89%, which is a clear sign that the days until the chopping block moment arrives are not that many left. The case for a dividend cut is also 'supported' by the previously mentioned PIK, which is a non-cash income, making the actual dividend coverage even worse. Furthermore, while FSK has some debt capacity available (debt to equity of 1.16x), the room is not that big to secure incremental NII per share growth just through debt-driven portfolio expansion.

Let's also remember that there are lagged effects from the Fed's base rate cuts in October, November and December that have not been absorbed in the Q3 data and will gradually percolate through Q4 and Q1, 2025 books. Namely, NII per share is very likely headed downwards.

So, this is, in a nutshell, the reason why the market is discounting FSK so heavily.

But having said that, I still think that the discount is too harsh and we should be able to profit nicely from FSK in 2026. Here is the thing.

Currently, FSK offers a dividend yield of 19.2%. Let's imagine that the Management will announce a cut by reducing the dividend by 40% in order to emerge out from the underfunded dividend's status (on a cash basis) and still leave about 10% of surplus NII per share generation that would allow to absorb the forthcoming pressure from lower interest rates. In that case, FSK's base dividend coverage would be close to 130% and 115% on a PIK adjusted basis, which is way above the sector average dividend coverage of 100%.

Because of the deep discount and elevated current yield, we would still be left with enticing current income streams of ~11.5%.

Now, the key is what happens with non-accruals going forward. In this context, the following comment by the Management in Q3 earnings call provides an important information:

We also believe it is helpful to provide the market with information based upon FSK's assets originated by KKR Credit. Nonaccruals relating to the 90% of the portfolio, which has been originated by KKR Credit and the FS/KKR Advisor, were 3.4% on a cost basis and 1.8% on a fair value basis as of the end of the third quarter. This compares to 3.8% on a cost basis and 2% on a fair value basis as of the end of the second quarter.

Many investors might not have noticed it, but the main source of FSK's exploding non-accruals has been the externally acquired portfolio, which has finally largely churned out, leaving smaller and smaller risk base from which unfavorable news could emerge. For instance, the current KKR Credit originated/underwritten portfolio non-accrual statistic is actually lower than for the publicly traded BDC sector average (closer to 2.5%). Finally, another element, which should help FSK is the lower base rate environment - i.e., positive for FSK investment companies, which now enjoy cheaper debt service than before the rates started to move down.

The bottom line

I want to make it clear that FSK is not your "buy and forget" case. I have not included it in my core BDC portfolio bucket, where there are such resilient gems as KBDC, Fidus Investment (FDUS) and Trinity Capital (TRIN). Instead, I have placed FSK in my tactical BDC portfolio bucket, which consumes much smaller percentage of the total exposure and where high risk, high reward plays can be made.

FSK's case really boils down to non-accruals. If FSK can finally showcase to the market that the worst days are over and that the current portfolio is largely cleansed from externally acquired trojan horses, then even after a dividend cut, I see significant upside from P/NAV convergence to the sector average (and all of this would be supported by post-cut double digit yield).

Seeing the recent data points on the portfolio and non-accrual front, I am willing to take that bet.

data.title: Apple Doesn't Need A Stronger AI Portfolio
data.description:
Apple Inc. (AAPL) has consistently been one of the largest tech companies in the world, worth more than $4 trillion. The company has consistently benefited from the dominance of its hardware platforms, although there are market concerns around its AI positioning. Despite that, Apple is continuing to be a substantial platform for AI with continued growth.

Apple Financial Results

Apple managed to earn impressive financial results in the most recent quarter.

The company crossed more than $100 billion in quarterly revenue a first for a non year-ending quarter (traditionally its largest). The company managed to achieve high single-digit YoY revenue growth, incredible given its scale, and it did that with total cost of sales at only $54 billion. That means roughly 52% of the company's revenue is cost of sales.

That's incredibly impressive for a primarily hardware company. At the same time, unlike other tech companies, Apple has comfortably managed investing in artificial intelligence, without blowing out any semblance of a budget. The company's R&D expenses have expanded by combined with G&A the segment is still at less than $16 billion / year.

The net result is after paying taxes, Apple had net income of $27.5 billion. That's more than $100 billion in annualized net income for the giant, net income that it continues to rapidly grow.

Apple Platform Dominance

At the same time, those who're afraid of the impact of artificial intelligence would do well to note Apple's status as a platform.

The top free iPhone app is currently ChatGPT. That application that everyone is concerned about replacing iPhone is being used through the iPhone. Similarly, the same people using ChatGPT online are using it through Mac. The same through coding tools like Claude AI etc. which are being run on the same laptops programmers were already using.

This is key in our view, because offloading compute for tasks to large language models, or LLMs, could potentially even reduce people's needs for beefy local compute enabling them to shift from Desktops to personal computing such as Laptops.

Apple Segment Breakdown

From a segment perspective, the Apple thesis remains strong with great performance across the board.

The company only saw revenue decline in the Greater China, where Apple is facing strong domestic competition. However, even here, the company's revenue decline was in the low single-digits. Everywhere outside of the market where there's concerted effort to limit the company in favor of domestic alternatives is seeing high single-digit to double-digit growth.

Apple is seeing strength in categories as well. While the iPad and Wearables categories remained flat, the company saw strong iPhone and Mac growth, the core categories that lead to follow-on growth with other categories. Apple's services business also remained incredibly strong. This is another sign of Apple's strength.

Apple Shareholder Return Potential

Since starting its shareholder return program, Apple managed to reducing its outstanding shares by 43%.

Apple has spent more than $700 billion to purchase these shares, or $64 / share. That's a quarter of current prices. Apple recently announced an extra $100 billion buyback after a $110 billion buyback last year. The company has a very modest dividend of <0.4%, but with buybacks total shareholder returns are roughly 3% annualized.

It's modest, but for a company growing at the high single-digits, it's still very respectable. The company's growth will enable increasing buybacks and shareholder returns and enable the company to expand into its P/E of ~30x that goes virtually entirely to shareholders.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is Apple's valuation. Apple is sitting right at a $4 trillion market capitalization, with $112 billion in TTM net income. That puts the company at a P/E of ~30x. In a difficult market, Apple needs continued net income growth to justify its valuation, and whether or not that happens remains to be seen.

Conclusion

Apple is one of the largest companies in the world with a $4 trillion market capitalization. The company has managed to shrink its shares outstanding by almost 50% over the past 13 years and it's focused on continuing to improve that. The company is spending almost $10 billion per month on share repurchases an indication of its massive scale.

The company is achieving high single-digit growth with strength on every segment of its platform. In addition, the company is the core platform that people are using for their AI usage, whether through ChatGPT or AI coding. Going forward, this makes Apple a valuable long-term investment. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: The AI Revolution: 3 Major Hurdles That Need To Be Overcome In 2026
data.description:
The AI Revolution has powered outsized gains within equities for three straight years now. The ten largest stocks in the market, now account for roughly 40% of total market capitalization. A concentration not seen since late in the Internet Boom to be noted.

Profit growth since Chat GPT debuted late in 2022 also has been dominated by AI related megacaps of Alphabet (GOOG) and Nvidia Corporation (NVDA). Average annual profit growth from rest of the "S&P 493" from 2023-2025? Roughly three percent. Something that hardly justifies the current market valuation of the overall market in my opinion.

The huge surge in tech spending spur by the buildout of massive AI data centers being constructed across the nation has become a huge driver of GDP growth.

Given the importance to the market and the economy of this spending on the AI Revolution, whether equities continue to advance in 2026 and economic growth remains robust is largely dependent on this key variable. So, what will determine if AI can continue to power equity gains and economic activity in the coming year? Here are three key factors investors should focus on in 2026.

OpenAI's Capital Raise:

No company has more instrumental to the surge in AI related tech spending in the U.S. economy as OpenAI (OPENAI), creator of Chat GPT. The company is bleeding cash and faces increasing competition from the likes of recently released Gemini 3 among other AI offerings.

Despite this, the company has made just over $1.4 trillion in commitments for future computing power. This amounts to roughly 100 times OpenAI's revenue for 2025. Based on these commitments, tech giants like Oracle (ORCL) are building out massive new capacity, much of which is being financed by debt.

OpenAI needs to raise substantial new capital to meet its current commitments obviously. There was an article last week in NY Post that there are talks going on between CEO Sam Altman and private investors to raise capital valuing OpenAI at a $750 billion valuation. This would come after insiders were allowed to sell over $6 billion worth of shares at a $500 billion valuation in October.

Reuters had previously posted that OpenAI was "laying the groundwork" for an IPO for the second half of 2026 at a valuation up to an eye popping $1 trillion valuation. If OpenAI could raise at least $100 billion in 2026 at or close to that valuation, that would go long way to alleviated investor concerns the company will be able to fund its current promises. Failure to do so, could have the opposite impact.

Power Generation Buildout:

The other front that needs to see significant progress if the AI Narrative is going to continue to hold and power the market in 2026, is the buildout of electrical generation capacity to meet the massive demand from these huge AI data centers being constructed throughout the United States.

And right now, there is a huge gap between the power this AI infrastructure will need and what is available/being added. How big is this gap? A recent Morgan Stanley projection (above) estimated new data centers will require an additional 72 gigawatts of electrical generation capacity by 2028. How much capacity is that? An AP1000 nuclear reactor from Cameco’s Westinghouse can provide just over 1.1 GW of electrical generation. It should be noted that 25 gigawatts of new electrical capacity currently being built to become operational over that time frame.

That leaves a large cap of 47 gigawatts according to Morgan Stanley. Nuclear has some promise. The notorious Three Mile Island reactor complex is even being brought back online. However, that new capacity will not be available to 2028 if everything goes to schedule. Smaller modular nuclear reactors are getting considerable buzz lately, but any new capacity on this front is unlikely until the early 2030s.

That largely leaves natural gas as the primary power source for all the new electrical generation capacity needed. In addition, more electrical generation capacity will also be needed to power the large ramp up of electrical vehicles that are projected to hit the road in coming years.

A MIT study in 2023 estimated that 4.4% of overall electricity in the U.S. was consumed by data centers in 2023. Another study projected that demand could grow to between 6.7% to 12% of all electricity usage by 2028. Already new data centers are driving much higher electricity costs in some regions of the country like Maryland and Virginia. Higher electrical costs is also one of many reasons why resistance to approve new data centers are rising in many locals.

Some new data centers are already sitting idle awaiting connection to the electrical grid. Considerable progress will need to be made on this front in 2026 to keep this factor from undermining the AI infrastructure build out.

Productivity Gains Materialize:

The last factor to keep an eye on in the coming year is if all the hundreds of billions of dollars of annual investment being made to build this AI infrastructure will drive the necessary AI related revenues and productivity gains to even begin to justify this investment. A recent report from McKinsey & Co. projected that over $6.5 trillion globally cumulatively by 2030 on AI. Just over $3 trillion on CPUs and other components that go into AI data centers.

Now, AI related annual revenues are expected to grow to $300 billion by 2029. That is impressive growth. However, it doesn't appear to justify the current levels of investments in my opinion. Will deployment of AI lead to huge productivity gains at companies?

There seems to be some considerable divergence on this front. A Wells Fargo equity strategist recently opined, 'While productivity for the S&P 500 has soared 5.5% since ChatGPT, it’s down 12.3% for the Russell 2000."

The flipside if AI does eventually lead to large productivity gains across the economy, it could result in substantial job losses and perhaps even a White-Collar Recession. A WEF survey that came out at the start of 2025 found 41% of companies globally expect to reduce their workforces over the next five years in roles where AI can automate tasks.

So, the question becomes can AI drive sufficient productivity gains and revenues to justify the huge AI investment but does so without triggering a huge rise in the unemployment rate? A delicate balancing act to achieve to say the least.

Given current market valuations and the challenges the continued AI Revolution faces, I am aligned to some degree with the conundrum around AI articulated by a Seeking Alpha follower on a recent article:

Either the AI narrative is correct and 25-50% of the workforce will be out of a job or it’s incorrect and we will see a big pullback in valuations of half the S&P500 index. There is no scenario in which this ends well - either AI success causes a major economic collapse or AI failure to live up to the narrative causes a large market crash."

How the three factors highlighted in this article play out in 2026 could go a long way to answer which path the AI Narrative move along in the years ahead.

data.title: Nvidia Is Breaking Out, Don't Get Left Behind
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) stock looks ready to move higher, as the fundamentals continue to be strong, the valuation is reasonable, and the chart may be starting to break out. To top things off, NVDA option prices are relatively low, making them attractive to buy.

Some investors worry about an AI bubble, but I don't think we're clearly in bubble territory. Valuations for some tech companies may be elevated but not egregious. "Bubble" is a big word that people throw around too often. The truth is, Nvidia has plenty of growth ahead with a palatable valuation and heavy profitability. The reason you hear Nvidia's name a lot is not because of a bubble, but because it's a juggernaut with $187.1 billion in TTM revenue.

And the recent news that the U.S. will impose new tariffs on Chinese semiconductors in June 2027 only strengthens the bull case. Competition is also something to consider, but it seems that at least for the short term, Nvidia can handle it.

I rate Nvidia stock as a Buy. The market doesn't listen to you if you think it's in a bubble. You need to listen to the market. Follow the upward trend for now.

A Revolutionary Company At A Reasonable Price

Nvidia is simply worth buying because it has momentum on its side as a revolutionary company, all while trading at a reasonable price. I'm not arguing that NVDA stock can never go down, but we need to realize that profitable leaders in quickly growing industries tend to trend higher over time, not lower.

Unlike Cisco Systems (CSCO), often cited as the posterchild of the dot-com bubble, Nvidia is not trading at a P/E ratio of ~200x. Instead, it's under 50x.

But the market is forward looking, so forward P/E multiples must also be taken into consideration. As pointed out by @dailychartbook on X last year (per data from LSEG Datastream and Yardeni Research), Cisco's forward P/E peaked at around 130x during the dot-com bubble.

By contrast, Nvidia's forward P/E looking one quarter out is around 40x, and 5 quarters out, it's around 25x, per analysts' estimates below.

But if we want to compare apples to apples with the Cisco valuation chart above, we need to see its forward P/E based on 12-month forward estimates. I got you. Its NTM price/normalized P/E ratio currently sits at around 27x. Not only is that much lower than Cisco's dot-com valuation, but it's also relatively low by Nvidia's standards, as the three-year mean is 37.27x, per the chart below.

At the same time, it's not fair to say that Nvidia is relatively undervalued just because its forward P/E is lower than its three-year average. That would be ignoring that the growth rate has since slowed since the insane growth that started a couple of years ago. Based on the numbers below, from Fiscal 2023 to 2024, diluted EPS grew by 600%, and from Fiscal 2024 to 2025, EPS grew by 147%.

Now, with EPS growth rates closer to 60%, it would make sense to see the forward P/E multiple being lower than usual. Nonetheless, my point is that the valuation doesn't scream bubble to me, even when factoring this in. Expected growth is still high, and the forward PEG ratio is 1.08x, which is not high at all and is lower than Nvidia's five-year average forward PEG of 1.62x.

Are EPS Estimates Even Reliable?

You may say that the valuation methodology above relies too much on estimates, and in a sense, you'd be right that we can't fully rely on estimates. At the same time, however, the trend has been that estimates are usually too low. In the past few years, trusting estimates has actually been the conservative move. In the chart below, you'll see that EPS estimates have been revised upward pretty consistently over the past few years.

I currently don't see any reason for Nvidia to suddenly start missing estimates badly and consistently, especially given what management said in the latest earnings call.

First, Colette Kress, Executive VP & CFO stated,

"We currently have visibility to $0.5 trillion in Blackwell and Rubin revenue from the start of this year through the end of calendar year 2026."

Then, Joseph Moore of Morgan Stanley asked:

I wonder if you could update us -- you talked about the $500 billion of revenue for Blackwell plus Rubin in '25 and '26 at GTC. At that time, you talked about $150 billion of that already having been shipped. So as the quarter is wrapped up, are those still kind of the general parameters that there's $350 billion in the next kind of 14 months or so? And I would assume over that time, you haven't seen all the demand that there is, there's any possibility of upside to those numbers as we move forward.

Interestingly, Kress stated that the company is on track to reach the $500 billion forecast but that there's "definitely an opportunity for us to have more on top of the $500 billion that we announced."

Here's a longer quote from Kress addressing that question:

I'll start first with a response here on that. Yes, that's correct. We are working into our $500 billion forecast. And we are on track for that as we have finished some of the quarters, and now we have several quarters now in front of us to take us through the end of calendar year '26. The number will grow. And we will achieve, I'm sure, additional needs for compute that will be shippable by fiscal year '26. So we shipped $50 billion this quarter, but we would be not finished if we didn't say that we'll probably be taking more orders.

For example, just even today, our announcements with KSA, and that agreement in itself is 400,000 to 600,000 more GPUs over 3 years. Anthropic is also net new. So there's definitely an opportunity for us to have more on top of the $500 billion that we announced.

The Chart Is Breaking Out

TrendSpider (a charting platform) stated it simply in an X post on December 23, saying "You're shorting? $NVDA is breaking out into Christmas and you're actually shorting?" I agree 100% with that statement and sentiment.

Take a look at NVDA's chart. It's in a clear uptrend, which already should give you an upward bias. The shorter-term trend has been more sideways in recent months, but the stock still made two higher lows recently (one in September and one in November). It's now breaking out of the diagonal trendline I drew, and the stock looks like it can test all-time highs and potentially go for its next leg higher.

The only caveat is that the week isn't finished yet, so that last weekly candle isn't yet completed, and the volume has also been relatively low due to this being a holiday week. I'd certainly be more confident if volume was higher, but I'll work with what I got.

Implied Volatility Is Relatively Low

It can make sense to buy call options when the implied volatility on those options is relatively low. According to Interactive Brokers, the 52-week IV rank for NVDA options is 3 (out of 100). That means the implied volatility is almost the lowest it has ever been in the past 52 weeks. It also has an "IV / HIST VOL" of 74.7%, meaning that the stock's implied volatility is 74.7% of its historical, realized volatility.

In other words, traders are pricing in lower than usual volatility. What this suggests is that options prices are not expensive at the moment, reducing the odds of "overpaying" like you might if you buy calls during a crisis moment when IV is extremely high.

China Tariff News Helps The Bull Case

This is not something to base the bull case on, but it certainly helps: news came out on December 23 that

"The United States will move to impose new tariffs on China’s semiconductor industry, introducing duties from June 23, 2027, on Chinese-made chips that are currently subject to zero tariffs, according to the U.S. Trade Representative."

This will make Chinese chips less competitive in terms of pricing, protecting Nvidia's moat. It seems that as long as long as the U.S. is in an AI arms race against China, they will continue to act positively in Nvidia's favor.

Risks To The Thesis

One of the biggest risks is that there's talk that Nvidia's moat is eroding or can erode. A reason for this is Alphabet's (GOOGL) own tensor processing unit ("TPU") chips that it's working on, which some argue will take demand away from Nvidia's chips.

As Deirdre Bosa stated on CNBC Television:

"Google's chip challenge to Nvidia -- it's moving from hardware to software. Nvidia's dominance with GPUs has always gone hand in hand with software needed to run them, that is CUDA...but now...Google is working to make its chips more adaptable, which will, in turn, make them more appealing to a wider swath of customers."

These chips were originally built for internal reasons but can possibly become good enough to sell in large amounts to other companies. At the moment, though, these chips haven't been able to "chip" away at Nvidia's moat, but they're worth monitoring. In the near term, Nvidia's management seems confident with its targets.

Another risk is the technical chart. Part of my thesis is based on the fact that Nvidia stock looks like it's breaking out. However, that's not something you can rely on with 100% accuracy, and a break of the $170 support level can signal a false breakout.

The Bottom Line On NVDA Stock

Nvidia looks set to move higher based on its reasonable valuation, strong fundamentals, and chart breakout. I think a way to take advantage of this is to buy relatively cheap call options right now, although shares work just fine too.

Personally, I don't think Nvidia is a bubble stock worth shorting or avoiding, as it's not at a crazy dot-com level valuation. Additionally, even if Google TPUs end up being a more notable risk down the line (which is debatable), that's likely not something to affect the company much in the short term.

The reality is that Nvidia is set to see crazy growth again in 2026, and it may even surpass expectations, as it always does. Just follow the trend until it breaks. NVDA gets a Buy rating from me.

data.title: JMUB: Low Risk Brings A Distinctly Modest Income
data.description:
The Machine Works as the Parts Interact

We grasp this, intuitively, about mechanical devices. Yes, of course, the output of the machine depends upon how the bits whizz around inside it. If we look at the bits and their whizz then we can have a darn good guess at the output of the machine.

Financial instruments are also machines. They've the added fun of uncertainty of course, but machines all the same. So, if we look at the internal parts, how they interact, then we can gain at least a good idea of what the output is going to be. In fact, we should look at these parts to understand what we're being asked to buy. This involves a certain amount of boredom but then we're talking about money here so that's worth it, right?

JPMorgan Municipal ETF (JMUB)

I'd not recommend this. Not because they're doing anything bad - something we do always have to at least think about with investments - but because I don't like what they're doing. If that's a distinction not yet wholly clear, their tactics, operations, they're all just fine — it's the strategy I don't like.

Of course others might be just fine with their strategy but then here we're supposed to be expressing opinions. What follows is an explanation of those moving parts, a couple of bits of my opinion and that all then leads to an explanation of why JMUB isn't for me and I'd not recommend it to you. But just to repeat: the not recommend is a result of my opinions — prejudices even — not because there's anything actually wrong here.

Bond Basics

For a full run through of bond basic try that section here. For a quick sprint, there are two risks in bonds, credit risk (not being repaid) and interest rate risk. We can trade off either of these for income: the more risk we are nominally taking then the greater the coupon/yield we will gain. Or, of course, the other way around, avoid some or more of those two risks and get paid less.

Municipal bonds carry the same interest rate risk as any other, less credit risk than corporates, more than Treasuries. The income is also tax free.

This is fine, obviously. But it produces a problem for us, as investors. For it means that there's no "one price" for a muni bond, Well, sure there is, the market price. But whether that price is worth it for an investor depends and it depends upon the marginal (i.e., on next $ of income) tax rate of the specific investor. In that sense there is no one price.

Tax Equivalent Yields

The way we get around this is to ask, well, how much would a taxable yield have to be to match this tax free yield? This again depends upon marginal tax rates so doesn't have the one answer but fortunately people publish tables of this for us:

The tax break is worth more to those on higher incomes, which seems sensible, logical at least. It's impossible to be categoric here as different people feel different levels of psychic pain at having to pay taxes. But the usual rule of thumb is that non-taxpayers should not worry about muni bonds. They're better off in taxable investments that they don't then pay tax upon. Highest rate taxpayers still looking for income should be definitely thinking about munis and the dividing line, well, probably around the 24% marginal rate.

JMUB Details

Now that we've got our rough rules, bond funds for diversification, the interactions of credit and interest rate risk with return, what it is that JMUB is doing?

They're going high credit quality. OK, this is a choice, it's a choice that means lower risk but also lower potential income.

Now there are some long tenor bonds in there but this is very definitely weighted towards the intermediate level, even a bit shorter than that. That's also just fine, it's a decision, one which means less interest rate risk and also a lower income. We can test the interest rate risk:

As interest rates rose after lockdown JMUB lost about 15% of capital value. Long tenor funds lost 30 and even 40%. That's just how interest rate risk works.

Actual top holdings are as follows:

The fund holds some 1,750 different issues/positions. Now, they're not saying they're actively chasing an index but that sort of number of positions is similar to one that is. Highly selective muni bond funds might have 300 or 400 positions.

But, OK. We've got our examination of the moving parts, we've got the moving parts. JMUB is going short to intermediate on tenor meaning less interest rate risk and thus less income. It's also going — comparatively, given what some other funds do — for very high ratings. This again means that a high income isn't going to be the result. Which it isn't, that trailing yield there is 3.2%.

Now, true, expenses are 0.18% which is pretty good. A totally programmatic index fund might be at 0.05%, a highly selective fund up at 0.7% or more. But fees aren't quite the point, it's cash in hand after fees that is.

And here's my problem. Yes, this is a low risk. By design, it's a high credit rating and low to intermediate tenor fund offering a return consistent with that risk. But I don't think that's the way to play the muni market.

Where Will JMUB Go in 2026

JMUB is going to track that municipal bond market really very well in 2026. That's the entire point of it having so many positions, being spread across tenors and credit risks. I might prefer being more selective, taking more nominal (but as I contend, no more real) credit risk to gain income. But that's me. And index matching fund like this is going to track the market. If interest rates fall — and I do expect they will but note expect, I've not a crystal ball — then there will be a modest rise in the capital value of JMUB. But it will be modest, and spread across the tenors. On the other hand, if rates rise then the capital loss will be modest for exactly the same reason.

The whole point of widely based index following funds is to track the general market, not take specific risks. JMUB is going to continue to do that.

Why I'm wrong

Some people will simply desire to reduce risk at the expense of income. That's a perfectly fine attitude and they should do as they wish. So my saying take more risk for higher income, for these people, would be incorrect.

The other thing is that the alternative I would suggest hasn't been, not fully, tested in recent decades. Yes, sure, we've had financial market problems but not a full blown and real economy recession. Relative credit ratings might be more correct when we do.

My Opinion

I am of the belief — hold the prejudice — that credit risk at the bottom end of the muni market is overpriced. As I explained here in detail but to put it in short. I think the risk in low credit grade munis is overpriced. That is, we get paid more income than the risk is really worth.

What this means is that I think the strategy of being only in high credit rating munis leaves money on the table (I'm being careful not to say "wrong" when I mean "less than optimal").

So, I end up thinking that iShares Short Duration High Yield Muni Active ETF (SHYM) for those who do not want to take much interest rate risk is a better idea (it pays 4.55% after all). Or, for those happy taking more interest rate risk Nuveen Municipal High Income Opportunity Fund (NMZ) which is paying 2% and more higher than JMUB. Both, of course, by taking more credit risk than JMUB but then that's my point, I think that's the sweet spot in the muni bond market.

Do please note, again, that JMUB is not doing anything "wrong" for the outcome from what they're doing is wholly consistent with the strategy they're following. I just believe — allow my prejudices to insist — that that's not the correct way to approach the municipal bonds market. Better is available from a different strategy.

data.title: HYD: Toward My Preferred Strategy But Not Quite There
data.description:
Understand the mechanics to understand the machine

How the whizzing around bits interact inside a machine is what determines the output of the machine. So, to grasp what the machine is going to do a knowledge of those moving parts is going to be useful.

Financial instruments are machines and they have those internal moving parts. Understanding what those parts are, how they interact, is going to enable us to predict the output. Of course, as financial markets always contain uncertainty we'll not predict exactly but get a darn good guide at least.

This does require a certain amount of boring looking at the details of cogs and levers of course, but we're talking money here so a bit of boring is OK, right?

VanEck High Yield Muni ETF (HYD)

I have opinions about the municipal bond market. Well, I have opinions about near everything in fact, I've spent a couple of decades being paid to write my opinions down too. But I have a couple of specific opinions about the muni bond market and the really relevant one here is that I think credit risk is overpriced at the bottom end of the market. We can thus pick up extra income and less than proportionate extra risk.

Now, note that's an opinion, even a prejudice, not a fact. But this then informs my view of HYD. They are following, in part, that strategy. This is then producing an above market return. As such I therefore recommend HYD to investors. Not as much as I recommend those fully following the idea but everything is relative, right?

Bond Basics

To understand the points we need a little primer on bond basics. For a fuller explanation read the bond basics section here. For those who already know, put very curtly here.

There are two major risks in bonds, credit risk - not getting repaid - and interest rate risk. We can increase the coupon/yield by taking more of those risks, reduce risk at the cost of income. Every bond fund is trading off those two because they are simply hard truths about the world. One of my contentions is that this risk/reward ratio is slightly off in the lower credit ratings of the municipal bond market. Thus there's an optimal strategy.

Municipal bonds are income tax free so the nominal return is lower than taxable bonds.

But this then produces a problem - how much is that taxfree income worth? For it depends upon the marginal (on the next $ of income) income tax rate of the investor which will, of course, differ.

Tax Equivalent Returns

The answer is that we ask, well, this is tax free, so, what would a taxable investment have to pay to produce the same in-pocket return? Not wholly easy as it depends, again, on the marginal tax rate of the investor but fortunately people publish tables of this for us:

It's not possible to be categoric since people hate paying taxes in different amounts, but the usual rule of thumb is that the lower rates mean munis don't make sense for non-taxpayers, the tax breaks make huge sense for highest rate taxpayers still looking for income and the dividing line is around, perhaps, the 24% marginal income tax rate.

To Add My Prejudice

This is something that I cannot prove so it's somewhere between an opinion and a prejudice. A full explanation is here, in the "How are they outperforming?" section. Put short and simple it's that I think the credit risk of low grade and unrated muni bonds is priced too high. Thus we can gain a return we get paid for a risk we're not taking. This is, to me, a good idea.

We can trade off credit risk with income, interest rate risk with income. We're talking about municipal bonds with their tax advantages. We've also that contention of mine, that risk is mispriced at the low end of the credit quality of the muni market. Those are the moving parts, so how does this version of the machine work?

HYD Moving Parts

The most obvious point from that list of top holdings is that the fund is very concentrated. 5% and more in the one position to me is concentrated. On the other hand it has 1,797 holdings in total, which means they holdings are very widely dispersed.

The answer here is this: "seeks to replicate as closely as possible, before fees and expenses, the price and yield performance of the ICE Broad High Yield Crossover Municipal Index (MHYX), which is intended to track the overall performance of the U.S. dollar denominated high yield long-term tax-exempt bond market." That is, they're tracking that index, which means being that very widely dispersed. The muni market as a whole is tens of thousands of issues, at least, but they're trading a subset of it, that high yield subset. And those top ten holdings are not of issues, rather of issuers. That's roughly what the high yield muni market looks like overall that is.

Other things we're interested in:

That's something I like and we'll come to that later, plus this:

Given the index they're trying to replicate is a long term one that gives us the long tenor of the portfolio.

So, from our varied explanations we can see what they're mixing and matching. They're going long tenor in search of yield but at interest rate risk. They're going low credit rating again in search of yield with, obviously, credit risk. Those are simply choices which we might feel happy about or not.

But then there's my insistence (opinion, prejudice) that credit risk is overpriced in munis. Thus we get to be paid for risks we're not taking by being in those unrated issues.

The costs here run at 0.3%. Which is higher than a pure index fund might be and lower than a more selective fund. But note that fees aren't the point, it's returns after fees that matter. The trailing yield is 4.63% — it's an ETF so there're no worries about return of capital to have to worry about. 4.63% is pretty good too.

So, on this basis I would recommend HYD to investors. Obviously, it's to a subset of investors. Those with incomes above the 24% marginal tax rate where munis make sense, and people still looking for an income as well.

But it's not a full-blooded recommendation. For I think it's possible to do better, to take the same idea further. This is also where I think index, or index following, funds tend to lag in performance.

The Future of HYD

Predictions, especially about the future, are difficult (not my joke, Nils Bohr) so this is tentative. But my gut feel is that interest rates are going to decline in 2026. Given the long tenor of the bond holdings here this will increase the capital value of HYD. But my gut feel probably isn't all that grand as investment advice which is why the recommendation is a hold, not an active buy.

Why I'm Wrong

One reason I could be wrong is simply that my favored strategy is wrong. We've not had a full on recession in many a year (we've had a financial crisis and lockdown but those are different things), so these bottom end credit ratings haven't really been tested.

The other is that many invest in munis for safety so telling them to go out along the risk curve doesn't suit their desires. And here, as everywhere else, the customer is always right.

My Opinion

I've long been skeptical of muni index funds. This one is replicating, not fully invested in one but still, and this is a good example of why. As with, say, my comments about SPDR Nuveen ICE Municipal Bond ETF (TFI). So, we've identified a winning strategy, this mispricing of low end credit risk. If we're index following then we're going to invest to the index, not according to the strategy. So, this index has perhaps 38% unrated, as with the fund. But if we like the strategy, why not go further? To, say, 49% unrated? As Nuveen Municipal High Income Opportunity Fund (NMZ) does. At about the same tenor as well, and NMZ is paying (after we adjust for RoC) 5.5 to 5.7%. That's a full percentage point higher, doing so by taking that strategy and really running with it.

So my opinion becomes yes, HYD is following my favored strategy in muni funds, going into unrated bonds so as to collect the excess premium offered against real risk (as I see it, obviously). So that's good and we can take their partial use of the strategy, and their partial outperformance, as being a test and partial proof of the idea. But they're not going hard enough at this strategy. At which point I prefer NMZ, or possibly Nuveen Municipal Credit Opps Fund (NMCO) as a recommendation.

Purely on the basis that if we think we've identified a winning strategy let's really go for it.

data.title: Par Pacific Holdings: A Risky Play In A Cyclical Industry
data.description:
Thesis

Investing in small refiners can be a risky endeavor. In a moment, margins are healthy. Those same margins can quickly shift to below break-even levels. This is a common theme among small refiners who struggle to stay competitive across the entire commodity cycle. Par Pacific (PARR) falls into this category with a total capacity of less than 200,000 barrels per day.

To the company’s credit, it produces well considering its relative size. PARR’s total production is less than 10% of large cap peer Valero Energy (VLO). As a result, I believe the recent 20% decline in share price is just the beginning. This retreat follows a significant run from earlier this year with the stock up over 100% YTD. Ultimately, there was bound to be a pull back. However, short interest levels remain elevated, indicating that the bearish sentiment is not fully priced in.

Crack spreads have declined significantly in December as seasonal weakness in demand has overtaken a strong October and November. This effectively shifts the refining market from above mid-cycle margins to below mid-cycle in just a matter of weeks. Unfortunately for small scale refiners, this generally also means shifting from cash generation to cash burn.

Over the long term, this equates to a neutral FCF profile and a rather expensive Price to FCF valuation level. Therefore, I rate PARR as a SELL. Investors should be cautious as bearishness persists, and seasonal headwinds dampen free cash flow generation.

Par Pacific Introduction

PARR is a small-scale refiner with operations focused on the pacific northwest. The company also operates a refinery in Hawaii, giving the company a rather unique operational footprint.

With other operations in Montana and Wyoming, PARR strikes me as a big fish in a little pond. The company’s refineries are located in low population density areas, making outside competition scarce. This gives PARR access to a captive customer base and allows for above average margin capture as a result.

In addition, the company also has complementary Retail and Logistics segments. These businesses give PARR vertical integration and a higher net margin over the fuels value chain.

These complementary businesses provide a stable source of EBITDA generation that complements the volatility typically found in the refining industry. YTD, these businesses have generated $216m in EBITDA, anchoring the company’s target debt level at 3-4x Retail and Logistics annual EBITDA.

Small Refinery Business Fundamentals

PARR has several advantages in its favor. In addition to the previously mentioned vertical integration, the company’s debt position is conservative at just 3.0x Retail and Logistics EBITDA. Further, due to the remoteness of the majority of its operations, the company has a competitive advantage by serving a trapped customer base. This is particularly the case in Hawaii where the company owns the largest refinery in the state.

However, offsetting these advantages are the company’s high operating costs. If you read my work on this sector, you will know that I am a stickler on this topic. I believe this is possibly the most important metric for a durable investment over the refining cycle.

In Q3, PARR set a company record for production cost per barrel. However, the company still ranks poorly compared to its competitors. PARR ranks in the bottom half of the peer group and $1.20/barrel behind the next closest competitor (Phillips 66 (PSX)).

This leads into why I am such a stickler on this topic. The higher an operator is on the cost curve, the more frequently they will operate at a loss. Generally speaking, I would estimate that the breakeven for the cyclical bottom lies somewhere around $6/barrel (reference the red line above).

For the high-cost providers, this creates a cyclical earnings profile. Cash generation occurs at the top of the cycle but turns to burning cash at the bottom. Investments in these producers should be counter cyclical, buying only when the industry is below mid-cycle conditions.

In contrast, the cost advantaged producers stay cash flow positive under all market conditions. These producers have less earnings volatility and generally have more stable stock prices.

Since PARR falls into the category of the former rather than the latter, I believe investors should be cautious. Seasonal softening of gasoline demand has already put significant pressure on crack spreads. In fact, PARR is likely operating at a net loss under current market conditions.

This exemplifies the risks I alluded to in the title of this article. The top part of the cycle is enticing, only for the cyclical swing to result in an eventual cash burn. As shown below, this is nothing new for PARR. Over the last five years, many quarters have alternated between positive and negative free cash flow.

In fact, this same behavior previously occurred this year. In Q1, PARR’s refining margins were nearly a $1/barrel less than productions costs. This resulted in a cash burn of nearly $175,000 per day from the company’s refining segment.

Adding in the capital-intensive nature of turnarounds and additional maintenance, it is easy to see how lean quarters can result in large cash outflows. As shown below, the large cash position built in 2022-2023 was quickly eroded in what was generally a challenging market in 2024 and early 2025.

These cyclical swings make investing in high-cost producers not for the faint of heart.

Why I Believe There Is More Downside For PARR

PARR posted a fantastic quarter in Q3 with an adjusted EPS of $5.95/share. This compares to an adjusted net loss of ($0.10/share) in the same quarter of 2024. While improved crack spreads certainly aided in this Y-o-Y shift, a significant portion of the improvement was associated with a one-time realization of a SRE (Small Refinery Exemption) impact to the tune of $195.9 million.

The SRE item was substantial to the company’s earnings and accounted for approximately 65% of the quarter’s profits. This creates a tough forward comparison, particularly when earnings are expected to decline in the seasonally soft winter market.

Market sentiment appears to also have shifted on PARR’s stock price. The stock currently has a short interest of 9.49%, indicating significant bearish sentiment.

Similar price action history can be seen in early 2024. Crack spreads peaked in March of 2024, as did PARR’s share price. The following nine months resulted in negative free cash flow and a 55% reduction in share price.

Thus is the life of a high-cost producer. As evidence, the chart below shows the cyclical change in share price of both PARR and PBF Energy (PBF) compared to large cap peers Valero (VLO) and Marathon Petroleum (MPC). Riding the cycle with small producers can make for some very high-highs and very low-lows. In the end, the large cap producers still came out ahead (and also pay dividends).

As a result, I would advocate for investors to take profits after an impressive run up this year.

Valuation

Because of the cyclical nature of PARR’s performance we will look at both long-term price to FCF and EV to EBITDA metrics.

As shown below, Price to FCF is quite expensive at 21 times free cash flow. This is a stark improvement from earlier in the year when peak valuation reached 600x despite the stock trading under $20/share. This again points to the cyclical nature of small refiners that alternate between positive and negative FCF.

Taking a longer perspective, we can see how EV:EBITDA fluctuated over the last five years. This snapshot encompasses both lean times in the post-COVID market as well as robust earnings in the wake of the Ukraine-Russian war.

The actual valuation number isn't as important as understanding how the valuation number fluctuates throughout the market cycle. In good times, the valuation looks cheap. In lean times, the valuation can be quite lofty.

This means that the real money is made by being counter cyclical. As shown below, buying during periods of low valuation have historically resulted in losses. This is the primary reason I keep my rating system for peers such as PBF and HF Sinclair (DINO) as counter cyclical as possible.

Risks

Although I am bearish on PARR's prospects, I would be remised to not mention other market dynamics occurring on the west coast. Phillips 66 most recently closed its LA refinery in October. In addition, VLO is expected to close its Benicia refinery in April of next year. Combined, these closures stand to remove over 300,000 barrels per day of capacity from the west coast market.

Although foreign imports are expected to fill the majority of these displaced volumes, west coast margins should rise. It is still too early to determine how this will affect mid-cycle pricing on the west coast. However, it is reasonable to assume that mid-cycle will move higher, leading to fewer periods of PARR operating at negative free cash flow.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. PARR has had a strong run as crack spreads have improved over the course of 2025.

2. Seasonal weakness has turned quickly from a strong October and November.

3. The market appears bearish on PARR with a high short interest. Similar market conditions in 2024 led to significant share price declines.

4. These significant changes in share price are attributed to the rapid fluctuations in profitability. As a high-cost provider of refined fuels, PARR cannot profitably produce over the entire refining cycle.

5. I remain countercyclical in my approach to investing in high-cost refiners. As such I recommend harvesting profits after a solid run in 2025. I rate PARR as a SELL.

6. Changes to the west coast market may improve the long-term fundamentals for PARR. I believe it is too early to draw conclusions on exactly how beneficial these closures will be on PARR's portfolio.
